title: Would You Do Me a Favor?

jokes.text: Apple sauce. No, no, I'm kidding. It says applause.
jokes.setup: Warm-up comic appears to misread the applause sign
jokes.punchline: Reveals he was joking about misreading 'applause' as 'apple sauce'
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay joke that establishes the behind-the-scenes setting. Functional but not memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Warm-up Comedian
dialogue.line: Apple sauce. No, no, I'm kidding. It says applause.

jokes.text: And now because he needs you more than you need him, larryyyyyy... Sanders!
jokes.setup: Traditional glowing talk show introduction
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest reversal acknowledging the host's desperation
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Hank's oblivious honesty. Subverts the expected intro format brilliantly while revealing the show's neurotic core dynamic.

dialogue.line: And now because he needs you more than you need him, larryyyyyy... Sanders!

jokes.text: you are an exceptionally hot crowd for the middle of the week.
jokes.setup: Lavish praise for the audience
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment that suggests weeknight crowds are usually terrible
jokes.explanation: Solid talk show host humor that reveals the artifice behind audience flattery.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: you are an exceptionally hot crowd for the middle of the week.

jokes.text: America's favorite couple, Tom and roseanne, have split up, but to prove that his career is not in trouble, Tom was recently seen dating tim allen.
jokes.setup: Celebrity divorce news leading to career concern
jokes.punchline: Absurd solution of strategic same-sex dating for career purposes
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed celebrity joke with good escalation from divorce to career desperation.

dialogue.line: America's favorite couple, Tom and roseanne, have split up, but to prove that his career is not in trouble, Tom was recently seen dating tim allen.

jokes.text: 76% of college seniors are sexually active, but only 50% of college freshmen are. So I have one message to all of you young people out there... stay in school.
jokes.setup: Statistics about college sexual activity leading to expected educational message
jokes.punchline: Subverts 'stay in school' message to be about sex rather than education
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection joke that takes familiar PSA format and subverts it effectively.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 76% of college seniors are sexually active, but only 50% of college freshmen are. So I have one message to all of you young people out there... stay in school.

jokes.text: You see the athletes preach that. You see the politician preach that. But only I, Larry Sanders, can say, 'stay in school. You may yet get laid.'
jokes.setup: Builds on previous joke about staying in school
jokes.punchline: Larry takes credit for being uniquely honest about the sexual motivation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that shows Larry's ego in claiming credit for honesty. Solid character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You see the athletes preach that. You see the politician preach that. But only I, Larry Sanders, can say, 'stay in school. You may yet get laid.'

jokes.text: Add to tonight's show a rudder and a sail, and it would have won America's cup. What is wrong with you? I thought it was more like the indy 500.
jokes.setup: Larry makes pretentious sailing metaphor about the show's quality
jokes.punchline: Hank completely misses the metaphor and offers an equally inappropriate racing comparison
jokes.explanation: Perfect character dynamic - Larry's pretentiousness met by Hank's obliviousness. Good absurdist escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Add to tonight's show a rudder and a sail, and it would have won America's cup.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What is wrong with you?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I thought it was more like the indy 500.

jokes.text: No, there was 3 points from outside! Swish! Nothing but net.
jokes.setup: Continuing the sports metaphor confusion
jokes.punchline: Hank now switches to basketball, still missing the point entirely
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Hank's complete disconnection from reality. Strong character work.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, there was 3 points from outside! Swish! Nothing but net.

jokes.text: Call Jason Alexander, and ask him if the... would you hold for a moment? Would you ask him if the laker tickets he has are floor seats? And don't let him know that I'm asking about it.
jokes.setup: Larry asking Beverly to call Jason Alexander about tickets
jokes.punchline: The transparent attempt to seem casual while being obviously desperate for good seats
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry neurosis - desperately wanting good seats but trying to maintain dignity. Realistic character behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Call Jason Alexander, and ask him if the... would you hold for a moment? Would you ask him if the laker tickets he has are floor seats? And don't let him know that I'm asking about it.

jokes.text: He said it would help if you knew the name of the song or the artist. I just can't get it. I heard it on the radio. Well, sing it. It's a woman... ♪ dum dum dum ♪ ♪ nuum nuuum ♪ you sing it to him.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to identify a song for the record store
jokes.punchline: His completely useless humming and passing the embarrassment to Beverly
jokes.explanation: Relatable cringe humor about trying to identify songs. Larry's selfishness in making Beverly deal with his embarrassment.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: He said it would help if you knew the name of the song or the artist.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I just can't get it. I heard it on the radio.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, sing it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It's a woman... ♪ dum dum dum ♪ ♪ nuum nuuum ♪

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: you sing it to him.

jokes.text: Someone is stealing money from my atm account. You got a doctor's prescription mixed up in here. What is this? 'Rectumin.' That sounds familiar. What's that for? For what it sounds like. That is for nasal congestion.
jokes.setup: Larry finds mysterious prescription with suggestive name
jokes.punchline: The medicine name suggests one use but it's actually for something completely different
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection based on pharmaceutical naming. The setup and payoff work well together.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Someone is stealing money from my atm account. You got a doctor's prescription mixed up in here. What is this? 'Rectumin.' That sounds familiar. What's that for?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: For what it sounds like.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That is for nasal congestion.

jokes.text: Who has access to your atm account? My business manager. And his assistant. My agent. Gardener and housekeeper. So... technically, your account is a big slush fund for the entire pacific rim.
jokes.setup: Larry listing people with access to his account
jokes.punchline: Artie's hyperbolic but accurate assessment of the security problem
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from specific people to 'entire Pacific Rim.' Good geographic absurdity and Artie's blunt assessment.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Who has access to your atm account? My business manager. And his assistant. My agent. Gardener and housekeeper.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: So... technically, your account is a big slush fund for the entire pacific rim.

jokes.text: Jason Alexander's seats are in the loge. The loge? Yes. In the loge. In the loge? The loge.
jokes.setup: Beverly reports ticket location
jokes.punchline: Larry's repeated incredulous questioning of the inferior seat location
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry status anxiety. The repetition shows his horror at the mediocre seats.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Jason Alexander's seats are in the loge.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The loge?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: In the loge.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: In the loge?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The loge.

jokes.text: Call him and tell him that... that you're busy tomorrow night but thank you anyway.
jokes.setup: Larry's reaction to the loge seats
jokes.punchline: His transparent lie to avoid admitting he doesn't want bad seats
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Larry's superficial priorities and social cowardice.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Call him and tell him that... that you're busy tomorrow night but thank you anyway.

jokes.text: These are too pastel-ish. Well, you're the one who said peach. No, I said, uh, plum. Tch. No, you didn't say plum. I said plum. I heard... OK. Well, maybe you said plum, but you said peach.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about tie colors
jokes.punchline: Circular argument about who said what color, showing Larry's inability to take responsibility
jokes.explanation: Realistic workplace dynamic showing Larry's petty control issues and inability to admit mistakes.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: These are too pastel-ish.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, you're the one who said peach.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, I said, uh, plum.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: No, you didn't say plum.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I said plum. I heard... OK. Well, maybe you said plum, but you said peach.

jokes.text: Tennis elbow. This is my tennis elbow ointment, right? Yes. Good. I wasn't gonna ask.
jokes.setup: Larry clarifying what the ointment is for
jokes.punchline: His relief that it's for a respectable ailment, referencing the earlier Rectumin confusion
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Larry's continued anxiety about the medical mix-up. Realistic neurotic behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Tennis elbow. This is my tennis elbow ointment, right?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Good. I wasn't gonna ask.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, your father called. What does he want this time? I don't know. Well, would you handle it for me? Larry, sooner or later you're gonna have to talk to the man. No, I don't. I'm getting relief already.
jokes.setup: Beverly urging Larry to talk to his father
jokes.punchline: Larry's 'relief' refers to the tennis elbow ointment, not resolving family issues
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection that shows Larry avoiding emotional issues by focusing on physical comfort. Good character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Oh, yeah, your father called. What does he want this time?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I don't know.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, would you handle it for me?

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Larry, sooner or later you're gonna have to talk to the man.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, I don't. I'm getting relief already.

jokes.text: He's going out tonight, right? Yeah. Dinner with Carrie Fisher. Remind him to wipe the rectumin from his elbow.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Larry's dinner plans
jokes.punchline: Artie's advice combines the prescription mix-up with practical grooming advice
jokes.explanation: Good callback that extends the medical confusion joke. Artie's practical but absurd advice is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: He's going out tonight, right?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Yeah.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Dinner with Carrie Fisher.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Remind him to wipe the rectumin from his elbow.

jokes.text: You realize this is only the second time that you've paid us the honor of a visit. That doesn't seem possible. Is that right? It is, isn't it? Yeah. And I have the photo to prove it... july 12, 1991. I remember.
jokes.setup: Hank noting how rarely Larry visits
jokes.punchline: The pathetically specific documentation of Larry's rare visits, including exact date
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his desperate need for Larry's validation shown through obsessive record-keeping. Cringe-worthy but believable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You realize this is only the second time that you've paid us the honor of a visit. That doesn't seem possible. Is that right? It is, isn't it? Yeah. And I have the photo to prove it... july 12, 1991. I remember.

jokes.text: I wouldn't know what to do if she left me. I couldn't brush my, uh... my teeth without her... literally.
jokes.setup: Hank praising his assistant Darlene
jokes.punchline: The absurd escalation to being unable to perform basic hygiene without help
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Hank's pathetic dependency. The escalation from general help to basic hygiene is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I wouldn't know what to do if she left me. I couldn't brush my, uh... my teeth without her... literally.

jokes.text: You're having money problems, huh? No. Just some of the numbers don't add up right. I don't need the details. Gimme the figure. 50,000. Damn it.
jokes.setup: Larry denying money problems while clearly having them
jokes.punchline: Hank's immediate offer of help reveals Larry was actually asking for money
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Larry's indirect way of asking for help. Hank's immediate willingness to help shows the relationship dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You're having money problems, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No. Just some of the numbers don't add up right.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I don't need the details. Gimme the figure.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Damn it.

jokes.text: You know... I'd like to do this, but I'm having... it's a little hard for me to swing right now. I'll tell you why. I'm having some plumbing problems down in the, uh... the look around cafe... and, um... just kidding. I don't need any money.
jokes.setup: Hank making excuses about why he can't lend money
jokes.punchline: Larry reveals he was never actually asking for money, making Hank's elaborate excuse unnecessary
jokes.explanation: Good reversal showing both characters' assumptions. Hank's ridiculous 'look around cafe' excuse adds to the humor.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You know... I'd like to do this, but I'm having... it's a little hard for me to swing right now. I'll tell you why. I'm having some plumbing problems down in the, uh... the look around cafe... and, um... just kidding. I don't need any money.

jokes.text: I hope you're not badgering our Larry. He's a grown man, sweetie. If he doesn't want to talk to his daddy, he doesn't have to. Do you think I enjoy chasing him down, Artie?
jokes.setup: Artie defending Larry's right to avoid his father
jokes.punchline: Beverly's pointed response about having to chase Larry down for everything
jokes.explanation: Good workplace dynamic showing Beverly's frustration with Larry's avoidance patterns. Artie's protection of Larry contrasts with Beverly's reality.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I hope you're not badgering our Larry. He's a grown man, sweetie. If he doesn't want to talk to his daddy, he doesn't have to.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Do you think I enjoy chasing him down, Artie?

jokes.text: Well, when I came here, when I took this job, I came here under the assumption that I was to do his schedule and return his phone calls, not play family therapist and do his tie shopping and pick the tomatoes out of his salad.
jokes.setup: Beverly listing her job responsibilities
jokes.punchline: The escalation from reasonable tasks to absurdly personal ones like picking tomatoes out of salad
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how Larry's neediness has expanded Beverly's job beyond recognition. The tomato detail is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, when I came here, when I took this job, I came here under the assumption that I was to do his schedule and return his phone calls, not play family therapist and do his tie shopping and pick the tomatoes out of his salad.

jokes.text: Darling, the job is to keep our little host happy... whatever that takes. Well, sometimes I think it takes too much. And do you think it would kill him if he could just say thank you just once? I thank you from the bottom of my heart for Larry's ma'am.
jokes.setup: Beverly complaining Larry never says thank you
jokes.punchline: Artie sarcastically providing the thanks Larry never gives
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie moment - gruff but understanding. His formal thanks on Larry's behalf highlights Larry's social failings while showing Artie gets it.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Darling, the job is to keep our little host happy... whatever that takes. Well, sometimes I think it takes too much. And do you think it would kill him if he could just say thank you just once?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I thank you from the bottom of my heart for Larry's ma'am.

jokes.text: I just kept calling you... Kramer out there. I'm... I'm sorry. No, that's all right. It's an easy mistake. It's just that I love the show so much, and I just keep getting you and your character mixed up. Yeah... ha ha ha. Well, th... that's weird, 'cause I... I play George on the show. Michael Richards plays Kramer.
jokes.setup: Hank apologizing for calling Jason the wrong character name
jokes.punchline: Jason's polite correction revealing Hank got it completely wrong - neither the right character nor actor
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment showing Hank's complete incompetence even when trying to be gracious. The meta-humor of referencing Seinfeld adds layers.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I just kept calling you... Kramer out there. I'm... I'm sorry.

dialogue.speaker: Jason Alexander
dialogue.line: No, that's all right. It's an easy mistake. It's just that I love the show so much, and I just keep getting you and your character mixed up.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yeah... ha ha ha. Well, th... that's weird, 'cause I... I play George on the show. Michael Richards plays Kramer.

jokes.text: He could have been calling you Elaine. That's what he called tim allen last week.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to make Jason feel better about the Kramer mix-up
jokes.punchline: Revealing Hank's consistent pattern of getting everything wrong with an even more ridiculous example
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation that makes Hank's incompetence even funnier. The image of calling Tim Allen 'Elaine' is perfectly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: He could have been calling you Elaine. That's what he called tim allen last week.

jokes.text: We had these crappy loge seats, and ovitz calls me, and he's giving us front row seats right next to nicholson. Di-did you say Wednesday? Really? No, no, it's Thursday. Oh. You know what? You're free on Thursday. Good. Oh. I'm in.
jokes.setup: Jason mentioning the upgraded Lakers seats
jokes.punchline: Larry's sudden availability when the seats become premium, with Beverly facilitating his hypocrisy
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the tickets subplot. Larry's transparent superficiality and Beverly's enabling create a perfect character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Jason Alexander
dialogue.line: We had these crappy loge seats, and ovitz calls me, and he's giving us front row seats right next to nicholson.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Di-did you say Wednesday?

dialogue.speaker: Jason Alexander
dialogue.line: Really? No, no, it's Thursday.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: You know what? You're free on Thursday.

dialogue.speaker: Jason Alexander
dialogue.line: Good.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh. I'm in.

jokes.text: What do you want me to do, take him to dinner, get him hammered, couple of hookers? No, no. That would be overkill. Just be yourself.
jokes.setup: Larry asking Artie to help with his father
jokes.punchline: Artie's escalation to extreme entertainment followed by Larry's deadpan response that just being Artie is enough
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for both. Artie's over-the-top solution and Larry's understated response that Artie's natural personality is entertainment enough.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: What do you want me to do, take him to dinner, get him hammered, couple of hookers?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, no. That would be overkill. Just be yourself.

jokes.text: Hey, Jerry! How the hell are ya? It's always a delight to greet my second favorite Mr. Sanders.
jokes.setup: Artie greeting Larry's father warmly
jokes.punchline: The backhanded compliment calling him the 'second favorite' Sanders
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - simultaneously warm and cutting. The 'second favorite' line is diplomatically insulting in his signature style.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Hey, Jerry! How the hell are ya? It's always a delight to greet my second favorite Mr. Sanders.

jokes.text: I guess beverly didn't know I prefer the window. Yeah, well, that'll knock it out of you, dad.
jokes.setup: Father complaining about getting an aisle seat instead of window
jokes.punchline: Larry's sarcastic agreement that an aisle seat is devastating
jokes.explanation: Good father-son dynamic showing Larry's inherited tendency to complain and his defensive sarcasm in response.

dialogue.speaker: Larry's Father
dialogue.line: I guess beverly didn't know I prefer the window.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, that'll knock it out of you, dad.

jokes.text: When you got money from me the other day, you didn't give anybody my atm password, did you? No, but you gotta admit that l-a-r-r-y is pretty easy to guess.
jokes.setup: Larry checking on ATM security
jokes.punchline: Phil pointing out Larry used his own name as the password
jokes.explanation: Perfect reveal of Larry's poor security practices. Shows his narcissism (using his own name) and lack of common sense.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: When you got money from me the other day, you didn't give anybody my atm password, did you?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: No, but you gotta admit that l-a-r-r-y is pretty easy to guess.

jokes.text: What, to Sanders? Hardy-har-har.
jokes.setup: Larry mentioning he changed his password
jokes.punchline: Phil correctly guessing the equally obvious new password
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Larry learned nothing. His predictable choice of last name shows consistent poor judgment.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: What, to Sanders? Hardy-har-har.

jokes.text: You're his sidekick? Yes, sir. Second banana. You know, I like to think of myself as second in command. Here you go. Like Ed mcmahon. Exactly like Ed mcmahon. That's a pretty sweet deal. Take home a big paycheck just for holding down a couch and selling crap.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to dignify his role as 'second in command'
jokes.punchline: Father's brutal assessment of sidekick work as just 'holding down a couch and selling crap'
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Hank's self-importance. Father's blunt assessment cuts through showbiz pretension with working-class directness.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You're his sidekick?
Yes, sir. Second banana. You know, I like to think of myself as second in command.

dialogue.speaker: Larry's Father
dialogue.line: Here you go. Like Ed mcmahon.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Exactly like Ed mcmahon.

dialogue.speaker: Larry's Father
dialogue.line: That's a pretty sweet deal. Take home a big paycheck just for holding down a couch and selling crap.

jokes.text: You know, for just a split second there, you reminded me of the old man himself. Henry Joseph Kingsley.
jokes.setup: Hank's response to being insulted about his job
jokes.punchline: His misguided attempt to find connection by comparing Larry's father to his own father
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's desperate need for connection even after being insulted. His obliviousness is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You know, for just a split second there, you reminded me of the old man himself. Henry Joseph Kingsley.

jokes.text: Beverly, I thank you from the bottom of my heart for handing Larry his undies. Larry thanks you, too. Don't you?
jokes.setup: Artie observing Larry's failure to thank Beverly
jokes.punchline: His pointed commentary calling out Larry's lack of gratitude while sarcastically providing it himself
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to Beverly's earlier complaint about Larry never saying thank you. Artie's intervention is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Beverly, I thank you from the bottom of my heart for handing Larry his undies. Larry thanks you, too. Don't you?

jokes.text: Your father's a big pain in the ass.
jokes.setup: Hank's assessment of Larry's father
jokes.punchline: His blunt, unfiltered conclusion delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his complete lack of filter delivering uncomfortable truth with maximum economy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Your father's a big pain in the ass.

jokes.text: I hate to agree with Hank, but I guess it's so. No matter what you do for my dad, I swear to God, there's never a thank you, like that would kill him.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about his father's lack of gratitude
jokes.punchline: The irony that Larry exhibits the exact same behavior toward Beverly
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character irony - Larry completely lacks self-awareness about his identical behavior. The parallel is perfectly constructed and deeply revealing.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I hate to agree with Hank, but I guess it's so. No matter what you do for my dad, I swear to God, there's never a thank you, like that would kill him.

jokes.text: All I know is it's the most frustrating thing in the world trying to please someone who doesn't appreciate anything you do. That is exactly right.
jokes.setup: Beverly describing her frustration
jokes.punchline: Larry's enthusiastic agreement while being oblivious that she's describing him
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony moment. Larry's complete lack of self-awareness while agreeing with a description of himself is expertly crafted comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: All I know is it's the most frustrating thing in the world trying to please someone who doesn't appreciate anything you do.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That is exactly right.

jokes.text: I know who stole your money.
jokes.setup: Building mystery about the ATM theft
jokes.punchline: Beverly's dramatic revelation that she knows the thief
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the revelation. Creates tension before the confession that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I know who stole your money.

jokes.text: I know. I took it. You took it. Yes, I took it. If you wanted to know why, why didn't you ask me, and I would have told you.
jokes.setup: The mystery of the missing money
jokes.punchline: Beverly's matter-of-fact confession that she took it, with the implication Larry should have just asked
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the theft subplot. Beverly's casual admission and suggestion that Larry should have just asked is perfect character logic.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I know. I took it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You took it.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yes, I took it. If you wanted to know why, why didn't you ask me, and I would have told you.

jokes.text: Because 3 months ago, you had me drive to encino looking for a paint chip from your decorator. I spent 3 hours in traffic, Larry. That's 3 hours for a paint chip, so I charged you.
jokes.setup: Beverly explaining why she took the money
jokes.punchline: The absurdly trivial nature of spending 3 hours in LA traffic for a paint chip
jokes.explanation: Great specific detail showing Larry's ridiculous demands. The paint chip errand perfectly encapsulates his petty perfectionism.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Because 3 months ago, you had me drive to encino looking for a paint chip from your decorator. I spent 3 hours in traffic, Larry. That's 3 hours for a paint chip, so I charged you.

jokes.text: It was on a Sunday, Larry! Yeah? So I charged you. And I charged you when you had me driving all over Los Angeles looking for peach-colored ties for you.
jokes.setup: Beverly continuing her justification
jokes.punchline: The emphasis on it being Sunday work, plus callback to the earlier tie color argument
jokes.explanation: Good escalation and callback that ties together Larry's petty demands. Sunday work detail adds to the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: It was on a Sunday, Larry! Yeah? So I charged you. And I charged you when you had me driving all over Los Angeles looking for peach-colored ties for you.

jokes.text: And I charge you for picking up your frozen yogurt at 7 every morning so that you can have the first batch out of the machine. Every morning?
jokes.setup: Beverly listing Larry's ridiculous demands
jokes.punchline: The obsessive detail of getting the first batch of frozen yogurt every morning, with Larry's shocked reaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect character detail showing Larry's neurotic perfectionism. The 'first batch' obsession is both specific and revealing of his control issues.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: And I charge you for picking up your frozen yogurt at 7 every morning so that you can have the first batch out of the machine. Every morning?

jokes.text: I'm tired of cleaning your ointment applicators.
jokes.setup: Beverly's growing list of inappropriate tasks
jokes.punchline: The gross personal detail of having to clean his medical equipment
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing how far Larry's boundary violations extend. References earlier medical subplot while being genuinely gross.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I'm tired of cleaning your ointment applicators.

jokes.text: I am tired of begging your father to come see the show so that you can feel good about yourself. Did you ask my dad to come and see the show?
jokes.setup: Beverly revealing she had to beg Larry's father to visit
jokes.punchline: Larry's focus only on the fact that she had to beg, missing the larger point about his neediness
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Larry's selective hearing and psychological neediness. His reaction reveals his priorities perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: I am tired of begging your father to come see the show so that you can feel good about yourself. Did you ask my dad to come and see the show?

jokes.text: disgruntled postal workers say they need the extra revenue because of the rising cost of ammunition.
jokes.setup: Postal rate increase explanation
jokes.punchline: Dark reference to postal worker violence
jokes.explanation: Sharp dark comedy referencing cultural phenomenon of postal worker violence

dialogue.line: disgruntled postal workers say they need the extra revenue because of the rising cost of ammunition.

jokes.text: Well, if I spent 30 years selling office equipment, I'd be bitter about damn near everything.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Larry's father being bitter
jokes.punchline: Artie's blunt assessment of why anyone would be bitter after that career
jokes.explanation: Good Artie moment with working-class wisdom. His matter-of-fact assessment of career disappointment rings true.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, if I spent 30 years selling office equipment, I'd be bitter about damn near everything.

jokes.text: This was returned by sinead o'Connor, sent back, uh... pasta sent back because there was a hair in it. Sinead o'Connor. There was a hair in it.
jokes.setup: Larry describing pasta sent back for having hair in it
jokes.punchline: The irony that bald Sinéad O'Connor complained about hair in her food
jokes.explanation: Clever irony joke playing on Sinéad O'Connor's famous baldness. Well-constructed with good economy and clear setup/payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This was returned by sinead o'Connor, sent back, uh... pasta sent back because there was a hair in it. Sinead o'Connor. There was a hair in it.

jokes.text: everything I got comedy-wise I owe to you. You're a terrific man, and I love you very much.
jokes.setup: Larry making a public declaration to his father
jokes.punchline: The awkward public over-compensation for his private avoidance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's tendency to make grand public gestures instead of dealing with private issues.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: everything I got comedy-wise I owe to you. You're a terrific man, and I love you very much.

jokes.text: and just say to my... assistant beverly, who I know is watching... I hope is watching... is probably not watching... but thank you, beverly, for everything you've done. I appreciate it, and thank you very much.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to publicly thank Beverly
jokes.punchline: His stream-of-consciousness undermining of whether she's even watching, making the thanks somewhat pointless
jokes.explanation: Good callback showing Larry finally thanking Beverly but in his typically neurotic way. The uncertainty about whether she's watching undermines the gesture.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: and just say to my... assistant beverly, who I know is watching... I hope is watching... is probably not watching... but thank you, beverly, for everything you've done. I appreciate it, and thank you very much.

jokes.text: This is a plate licked clean by roseanne.
jokes.setup: Larry building up to a big reveal in the food bit
jokes.punchline: The image of Roseanne Barr licking a plate completely clean
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy bit with celebrity reference. The image is memorable if somewhat mean-spirited.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: This is a plate licked clean by roseanne.

jokes.text: He loved it. But he left. You're kidding. He had to catch an early plane to pittsburgh. He sends his love. Left right after the marilu henner segment.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about his father's reaction
jokes.punchline: The deflating news that father left early, specifically after a boring segment
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Larry's hopes. The specific detail about leaving after Marilu Henner adds to the disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: He loved it. But he left.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You're kidding.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: He had to catch an early plane to pittsburgh. He sends his love. Left right after the marilu henner segment.

jokes.text: Steve called, and he can't go. He's sick. So I asked Julia roberts to come along. Boing! Rah! See ya, kid.
jokes.setup: Jason explaining Steve Guttenberg can't make the Lakers game
jokes.punchline: The casual mention of Julia Roberts as a replacement, showing the different levels of Hollywood access
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the casual nature of A-list connections. Makes Larry's exclusion more painful.

dialogue.speaker: Jason Alexander
dialogue.line: Steve called, and he can't go. He's sick. So I asked Julia roberts to come along. Boing! Rah! See ya, kid.

jokes.text: I wanted blueberry, and, uh, there's no pecans... but thanks.
jokes.setup: Paula bringing Larry frozen yogurt
jokes.punchline: Larry's inability to just accept a kind gesture without finding fault
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry moment showing his inability to accept kindness without criticism. Undercuts any growth from the Beverly thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I wanted blueberry, and, uh, there's no pecans... but thanks.

jokes.text: It's Leno now, right?
jokes.setup: Larry mentioning his new ATM password
jokes.punchline: Paula correctly guessing it's his rival's name, showing his obsession
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending callback showing Larry's unchanged nature. Using his rival's name as password is peak Larry psychology.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: It's Leno now, right?

tmdb_overview: Larry's dad pays a visit to California, but Larry doesn't want to see him, so he adds that little chore to the things an already fed-up Beverly has to cope with.
